title: Accessibility 101: The 30-Second Alt Text Rule
content:
# Accessibility 101: The 30-Second Alt Text Rule

**Role:** All Staff | **Time:** 5 Minutes | **Difficulty:** Beginner

---

## 🛑 Stop: Don't Hit "Send" Yet

Every image you publish—in an email, a Word doc, or on the web—needs a text alternative. Without it, a blind resident using a screen reader will just hear "Image 4829.jpg."

---

## The Rule

**"If this image disappeared completely, what text would I need to put here to keep the meaning intact?"**

---

## The 3 Categories

### 1. Information 🧠
**The image tells you something** (a chart, a photo of a broken sidewalk).

**Action:** Describe the information.

**Example:**
- ❌ Bad: "Chart"
- ✅ Good: "Bar chart showing water usage decreased 15% in 2024"

---

### 2. Decor 🎨
**The image is just for vibes** (swirls, generic stock photo).

**Action:** Mark as "Decorative" (Null).

**Example:**
- Decorative border graphics
- Background patterns
- Generic stock photos that don't add information

---

### 3. Link 🔗
**The image is a button.**

**Action:** Describe the destination (e.g., "Facebook Page").

**Example:**
- ❌ Bad: "Icon"
- ✅ Good: "Visit our Facebook page"

---

## Quick Practice

**Image:** A photo of a "Road Closed" sign on Ruxton Avenue.

**What's the alt text?**

<details>
<summary>Click to see answer</summary>

**Answer:** "Road Closed sign on Ruxton Avenue"

**Why:** It's informational—tells residents about a road closure. The alt text should convey the same information a sighted person would get.
</details>

---

## How to Add Alt Text

### In Word/PowerPoint
1. Right-click the image
2. Select "Edit Alt Text"
3. Type your description
4. If decorative, check "Mark as decorative"

### In Outlook
1. Right-click the image
2. Select "View Alt Text"
3. Add description in the sidebar

### In SharePoint
1. Click the image
2. Click the pencil icon (Edit)
3. Add "Alternative text" in the properties panel

---

## Remember

**No alt text = No access for blind users.**

It takes 10 seconds. Make it count.

title: Accessible Excel Spreadsheets
content:
# Module 102: Accessible Excel Spreadsheets

**Role:** All Staff | **Time:** 7 Minutes | **Difficulty:** Beginner

---

## Why Excel Accessibility Matters

Excel spreadsheets often contain critical data, budgets, and schedules. If a spreadsheet isn't accessible, users with visual impairments or those using screen readers cannot navigate the data or understand the context of the numbers.

---

## The Built-In Accessibility Checker

**Your Secret Weapon:**

1. Click the **Review** tab
2. Click **Check Accessibility**
3. Fix each item in the sidebar
4. Re-check until you see "No issues found"

**Use it before sharing ANY spreadsheet.**

---

## The 5 Essential Rules

### 1. Identify Header Rows and Columns

**How:**
1. Highlight your data table
2. Go to Insert → Table
3. Make sure "My table has headers" is checked

**Why:** Screen readers use table headers to provide context for each cell's data.

---

### 2. Give Sheet Tabs Unique Names

**❌ Wrong:**
- Sheet1, Sheet2, Sheet3

**✅ Right:**
- 2024 Budget, Q1 Expenses, Employee Roster

**Why:** Blank or default sheet names tell users nothing about what information is on that worksheet.

---

### 3. Add Alt Text to Charts and Images

**How:**
1. Right-click the chart or image
2. Select "Edit Alt Text"
3. Describe the main takeaway of the chart

**Example:**
- ❌ Bad: "Chart 1"
- ✅ Good: "Bar chart showing a 15% decrease in water usage from 2023 to 2024"

---

### 4. Avoid Blank Rows and Columns for Formatting

**❌ Wrong:**
- Leaving column B blank just to create visual space

**✅ Right:**
- Resize the width of Column A or Column C instead

**Why:** Screen readers might tell a user "Blank" and lead them to believe they have reached the end of the data.

---

### 5. Keep Tables Simple

**Do:**
- Use a simple grid structure

**Don't:**
- Merge cells or split cells

**Why:** Merged cells disrupt the reading order for screen reading software, making it impossible to determine which header applies to which cell.

---

## Color and Contrast

### Don't Rely on Color Alone

**❌ Wrong:**
- Formatting negative numbers in red only

**✅ Right:**
- Formatting negative numbers with a minus sign AND in red

**Why:** Colorblind users can't distinguish red from green or black.

---

## Quick Accessibility Checklist

Before sharing any Excel document:
- [ ] Used real Tables with defined headers
- [ ] Named all Worksheet tabs clearly
- [ ] Added alt text to all charts and images
- [ ] Removed unnecessary blank rows/columns
- [ ] Unmerged any merged cells
- [ ] Checked color contrast
- [ ] Ran Accessibility Checker
- [ ] Fixed all errors

---

## Resources

- **Built-in tool:** Review tab → Check Accessibility
- **Microsoft guide:** [Make your Excel documents accessible](https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/make-your-excel-documents-accessible-to-people-with-disabilities-6cc05fc5-1314-48b5-8eb3-683e49b3e593)

title: Procurement & Vendor Accessibility
content:
# Module 103: Procurement & Vendor Accessibility

**Role:** Purchasing, Managers, IT | **Time:** 7 Minutes | **Difficulty:** Intermediate

---

## Why Vendor Accessibility Matters

When we purchase third-party software, tools, or web services, we are responsible for ensuring that those products are accessible to our staff and the public. Under the ADA, government entities cannot contract away their accessibility obligations.

---

## The 4 Essential Rules for Accessible Procurement

### 1. Include Accessibility in RFPs and Contracts

**What to do:**
Always include specific accessibility requirements in your Requests for Proposals (RFPs) and vendor contracts.

**Required Language:**
- Require compliance with **WCAG 2.1 Level AA**.
- Include clauses that hold the vendor responsible for fixing accessibility defects at their own expense.

---

### 2. Request and Review VPATs

A VPAT (Voluntary Product Accessibility Template) is a document created by the vendor that explains how well their product conforms to accessibility standards.

**What to do:**
1. Always ask vendors for their latest VPAT or ACR (Accessibility Conformance Report).
2. Review the VPAT. "Supports with Exceptions" means there are accessibility bugs. Do not accept a VPAT that is completely blank or claims "Not Applicable" for web interfaces.
3. If they don't have a VPAT, it is a red flag that accessibility was not a priority.

---

### 3. Ask Questions During Demos

When vendors demonstrate their software, ask them to prove it's accessible.

**Questions to ask:**
- "Can you navigate this entire workflow using just the keyboard (without a mouse)?"
- "Has this product been tested with screen readers like JAWS or NVDA?"
- "Do all of your videos have closed captions?"

---

### 4. Plan for Accommodations

Even the best products might have some accessibility gaps. 

**What to do:**
If you must purchase a product with known accessibility issues (because no accessible alternative exists), you must create a proactive plan (an "Equally Effective Alternate Access Plan") for how you will accommodate users who cannot use the product.

---

## The Vendor Evaluation Checklist

Before signing a contract for digital services or software:
- [ ] Accessibility requirements (WCAG 2.1 AA) are explicitly stated in the RFP.
- [ ] Vendor provided a valid VPAT/ACR.
- [ ] Vendor demonstrated keyboard accessibility during the demo.
- [ ] Contract includes language requiring the vendor to fix accessibility bugs.
- [ ] If accessibility exceptions exist, an accommodation plan is in place.

---

## Real-World Example

### ❌ Before (Inaccessible Procurement)
Purchasing a new HR portal just because it has the best price, without asking about accessibility. Once deployed, an employee who uses a screen reader cannot submit their timesheet. The city is now liable.

### ✅ After (Accessible Procurement)
Asking for a VPAT during the RFP process. The vendor provides one and demonstrates that their portal can be completely operated via keyboard. The contract states the vendor will resolve any newly discovered accessibility issues within 30 days.

---

## Resources

- **Section508.gov:** [Buy Accessible IT](https://www.section508.gov/buy/)
- **W3C:** [Procuring Accessible IT](https://www.w3.org/WAI/procure/)

title: Accessible Email in Outlook
content:
# Module 106: Accessible Email in Outlook

**Role:** All Staff | **Time:** 6 Minutes | **Difficulty:** Beginner

---

## The "Big 3" Checklist

### 1. Give Your Email "Bones" (Headings)

Sighted users scan big bold text. Blind users scan by Headings.

**Do:** Use the "Format Text" ribbon and select **Heading 1** for main topics.

**Don't:** Just use Bold/Underline.

#### Why It Matters
Screen readers can jump between headings, letting users navigate long emails quickly. Without proper headings, they have to listen to every single word.

#### How to Do It
1. Highlight your section title
2. Go to **Format Text** tab
3. Click **Styles** dropdown
4. Select **Heading 1** (or Heading 2 for subsections)

**Example:**
```
Heading 1: Community Meeting Schedule
Heading 2: February Events
Heading 2: March Events
```

---

### 2. The Link Test

**❌ Fail:** "Click here." (Where does it go? No idea.)

**✅ Win:** "Read the 2024 Water Quality Report." (Clear context).

#### Why It Matters
Screen reader users often navigate by links. When they hear "click here, click here, click here," they have no idea what any of them do.

#### The Rule
**Your link text should make sense out of context.**

#### Examples

**Bad:**
- "Click here for more information"
- "Learn more"
- "Read this"

**Good:**
- "View the City Council meeting agenda"
- "Download the 2024 budget summary (PDF)"
- "Register for the community cleanup event"

---

### 3. Check It

Use the **Accessibility Checker** button in the **Review** tab before sending any city-wide email. It will catch missed Alt Text and bad contrast automatically.

#### How to Use It
1. Click the **Review** tab
2. Click **Check Accessibility**
3. Review the sidebar for issues
4. Fix each item before sending

#### Common Issues It Catches
- Missing alt text on images
- Low contrast text (light gray on white)
- Missing email subject
- Tables without header rows
- Unclear link text

---

## Bonus Tips

### Use Lists
When you have multiple items, use the bullet or numbered list buttons. Screen readers announce "List with 5 items," helping users understand structure.

### Avoid "Image Only" Emails
Always include text content. Some users disable images for security or data reasons.

### Test Your Contrast
Light gray text might look sleek, but if it's hard to read, it fails accessibility. Stick to dark text on light backgrounds.

---

## Real-World Example

### ❌ Before (Inaccessible)
```
**Important Info**

Click here to see the schedule.

[Image of calendar]

For questions, click here.
```

### ✅ After (Accessible)
```
Heading 1: Community Center Schedule Changes

View the updated February schedule (PDF).

[Image: Calendar showing Community Center closed Feb 15-17 for maintenance]

Contact the Community Center with questions.
```

---

## Remember

**Accessible email = More residents reached.**

These three steps take 30 seconds and ensure everyone can access your message.

title: Creating Accessible PDFs
content:
# Module 107: Creating Accessible PDFs

**Role:** Content Creators | **Time:** 8 Minutes | **Difficulty:** Intermediate

---

## Why PDFs Are a Problem

PDFs are the #1 accessibility complaint for government websites. A PDF created by just "printing to PDF" is essentially a picture of text—completely unusable for screen readers.

---

## The Golden Rule

**If you can avoid publishing a PDF, do it.**

Post content as a web page instead. PDFs should only be used for:
- Official forms that need signatures
- Documents that must be printed exactly as designed
- Legal documents requiring specific formatting

---

## The 3-Step Accessible PDF Process

### Step 1: Start with Structure

**In Word/PowerPoint:**
- Use built-in Heading styles (Heading 1, Heading 2, etc.)
- Use the built-in List buttons for bullets and numbering
- Use the Table tools to create tables (not tabs or spaces)
- Add alt text to all images

**Why:** These create the "bones" that screen readers need to navigate.

---

### Step 2: Export Properly

**In Microsoft Word:**
1. File → Save As → PDF
2. Click **Options**
3. ✅ Check "Document structure tags for accessibility"
4. ✅ Check "Create bookmarks"
5. Save

**In Adobe Acrobat Pro:**
1. Use "Export PDF" (not "Print to PDF")
2. Ensure "Create Tagged PDF" is checked

---

### Step 3: Check It

**Use Adobe Acrobat Pro's Accessibility Checker:**
1. Tools → Accessibility → Full Check
2. Fix any errors (missing tags, reading order, alt text)
3. Re-check until it passes

**No Adobe Acrobat Pro?**
- Use the free [PAC 2024 checker](https://pdfua.foundation/en/pdf-accessibility-checker-pac)
- Or use Word's built-in Accessibility Checker before exporting

---

## Common PDF Accessibility Errors

### ❌ Error 1: Scanned Documents
**Problem:** Scanning a paper document creates an image, not text.

**Fix:** Use OCR (Optical Character Recognition) in Adobe Acrobat:
- Tools → Enhance Scans → Recognize Text

---

### ❌ Error 2: No Reading Order
**Problem:** Screen readers don't know what order to read content.

**Fix:** In Adobe Acrobat Pro:
- Tools → Accessibility → Reading Order
- Drag boxes to set correct order

---

### ❌ Error 3: Form Fields Without Labels
**Problem:** A form with blank boxes but no labels for screen readers.

**Fix:** In Adobe Acrobat Pro:
- Tools → Prepare Form
- Add tooltips to each field describing what goes there

---

## The "Print to PDF" Test

**Try this:**
1. Open your PDF
2. Try to select and copy text
3. Can you? Good sign.
4. Can't you? It's probably an image. ❌

---

## Real-World Example

### ❌ Before (Inaccessible)
- Created in Word with no headings
- Printed to PDF from browser
- Scanned document with no OCR
- Result: Screen reader says "Blank page"

### ✅ After (Accessible)
- Created in Word with Heading 1, Heading 2 styles
- All images have alt text
- Exported using "Save As PDF" with tags enabled
- Checked in Adobe Acrobat Pro
- Result: Screen reader can navigate by headings and read all content

---

## Quick Checklist

Before publishing any PDF:
- [ ] Used proper headings in source document
- [ ] Added alt text to all images
- [ ] Exported with "Document structure tags" enabled
- [ ] Ran accessibility checker
- [ ] Fixed all errors
- [ ] Tested with screen reader or tab navigation

---

## When to Use Web Pages Instead

**Use a web page if:**
- Content will be read online (not printed)
- Content needs to be searchable
- Content will be updated frequently
- You want the best accessibility

**Use a PDF if:**
- Official form requiring signature
- Legal document with specific layout requirements
- Content must be printed exactly as designed

---

## Resources

- **Free PDF Checker:** [PAC 2024](https://pdfua.foundation/en/pdf-accessibility-checker-pac)
- **Adobe Acrobat Pro:** Available through IT (request access)
- **Training:** Adobe's free [Accessible PDF tutorials](https://www.adobe.com/accessibility/pdf/pdf-accessibility-overview.html)

---

## Remember

**An inaccessible PDF = A locked door for residents with disabilities.**

Take the extra 5 minutes to do it right.

title: Accessible Word Documents
content:
# Module 108: Accessible Word Documents

**Role:** All Staff | **Time:** 7 Minutes | **Difficulty:** Beginner

---

## Why Word Accessibility Matters

Word documents are shared with residents, posted online, and converted to PDFs. If the Word doc isn't accessible, nothing created from it will be either.

---

## The Built-In Accessibility Checker

**Your Secret Weapon:**

1. Click the **Review** tab
2. Click **Check Accessibility**
3. Fix each item in the sidebar
4. Re-check until you see "No issues found"

**Use it before sharing ANY document.**

---

## The 5 Essential Rules

### 1. Use Heading Styles (Not Just Big Bold Text)

**❌ Wrong:**
- Typing text and making it bold and size 18

**✅ Right:**
- Highlight text → Home tab → Styles → Heading 1

**Why:** Screen readers use headings to navigate. "Bold text" means nothing to them.

---

### 2. Use Real Lists (Not Dashes or Asterisks)

**❌ Wrong:**
```
- Item one
- Item two
- Item three
```

**✅ Right:**
- Use the Bullets button in the Home tab

**Why:** Screen readers announce "List with 3 items" so users know the structure.

---

### 3. Add Alt Text to Images

**How:**
1. Right-click image
2. "Edit Alt Text"
3. Describe the information the image conveys
4. If decorative, check "Mark as decorative"

**Example:**
- ❌ Bad: "Image1.jpg"
- ✅ Good: "City Council members at ribbon cutting ceremony for new accessible playground"

---

### 4. Use Tables Correctly

**Do:**
- Insert → Table (use the table tools)
- Designate the first row as a header row
- Keep tables simple (avoid merged cells if possible)

**Don't:**
- Use tabs or spaces to create columns
- Use tables for layout (use columns instead)

**Why:** Screen readers read tables cell by cell and announce headers.

---

### 5. Make Links Descriptive

**❌ Wrong:**
- "Click here for more information"
- "Read more"

**✅ Right:**
- "Read the 2024 Community Survey Results"
- "Download the Parks and Recreation Master Plan (PDF)"

**Why:** Screen reader users navigate by links. "Click here" tells them nothing.

---

## Color and Contrast

### Don't Rely on Color Alone

**❌ Wrong:**
- "Items in red are required"

**✅ Right:**
- "Items marked with an asterisk (*) are required"
- Use color AND another indicator (bold, asterisk, icon)

**Why:** Colorblind users can't distinguish red from green.

---

### Check Your Contrast

**Rule:** Text must have sufficient contrast against background.

**Good combinations:**
- Black text on white background
- Dark blue text on white background
- White text on dark blue background

**Bad combinations:**
- Light gray text on white background
- Yellow text on white background
- Red text on green background

---

## Document Properties

**Set these before sharing:**

1. File → Info → Properties
2. Add:
   - **Title:** Document name
   - **Author:** Department name
   - **Subject:** Brief description

**Why:** Screen readers announce this info when the document opens.

---

## Language Settings

If your document includes Spanish or other languages:

1. Highlight the foreign language text
2. Review tab → Language → Set Proofing Language
3. Select the correct language

**Why:** Screen readers will pronounce it correctly.

---

## Quick Accessibility Checklist

Before sharing any Word document:
- [ ] Used Heading styles (not just bold)
- [ ] Used List buttons (not dashes)
- [ ] Added alt text to all images
- [ ] Used Table tools for tables
- [ ] Made links descriptive
- [ ] Checked color contrast
- [ ] Ran Accessibility Checker
- [ ] Fixed all errors

---

## Real-World Example

### ❌ Before (Inaccessible)
```
COMMUNITY MEETING SCHEDULE (Bold, Size 18)

* February 15 - Budget Workshop
* March 3 - Public Hearing

For more info click here.
```

**Problems:**
- "COMMUNITY MEETING SCHEDULE" is just bold text, not a heading
- Asterisks instead of real list
- "Click here" link

---

### ✅ After (Accessible)
```
Community Meeting Schedule (Heading 1)

• February 15 - Budget Workshop
• March 3 - Public Hearing

View the full City Council meeting calendar.
```

**Fixed:**
- Heading 1 style applied
- Real bullet list
- Descriptive link text

---

## Bonus: Columns vs. Tables

**Use Columns for:**
- Newsletter layouts
- Side-by-side text

**Use Tables for:**
- Data with rows and columns
- Information that needs headers

**How to add columns:**
- Layout tab → Columns → Two (or Three)

---

## Remember

**Accessible Word docs = Accessible everything.**

PDFs, web content, and printed materials all start with Word. Get it right at the source.

---

## Resources

- **Built-in tool:** Review tab → Check Accessibility
- **Microsoft guide:** [Make your Word documents accessible](https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/make-your-word-documents-accessible-d9bf3683-87ac-47ea-b91a-78dcacb3c66d)

title: Accessible PowerPoint Presentations
content:
# Module 109: Accessible PowerPoint Presentations

**Role:** All Staff | **Time:** 7 Minutes | **Difficulty:** Beginner

---

## Why PowerPoint Accessibility Matters

Presentations are shared at public meetings, posted online, and sent to residents. An inaccessible PowerPoint excludes people who use screen readers or have visual impairments.

---

## The 6 Essential Rules

### 1. Use Built-In Slide Layouts

**❌ Wrong:**
- Starting with a blank slide and adding text boxes

**✅ Right:**
- Home tab → Layout → Choose a layout (Title Slide, Title and Content, etc.)

**Why:** Built-in layouts have proper reading order for screen readers.

---

### 2. Use Unique Slide Titles

**Every slide needs a title**, even if you hide it visually.

**How to hide a title:**
1. Add the title text
2. Right-click the title placeholder
3. "Format Shape" → Set transparency to 100%

**Why:** Screen reader users navigate by slide titles. "Slide 1, Slide 2, Slide 3" is useless.

---

### 3. Add Alt Text to Images, Charts, and Graphics

**How:**
1. Right-click the image/chart
2. "Edit Alt Text"
3. Describe what the visual conveys

**Examples:**

**For a photo:**
- ❌ Bad: "Photo"
- ✅ Good: "Mayor Johnson presenting certificate to volunteer of the year"

**For a chart:**
- ❌ Bad: "Chart"
- ✅ Good: "Bar chart showing water usage decreased 15% from 2023 to 2024"

**For decorative graphics:**
- Check "Mark as decorative"

---

### 4. Use Sufficient Color Contrast

**Rule:** Text must be readable against the background.

**Good combinations:**
- Dark text on light background
- Light text on dark background

**Bad combinations:**
- Light gray text on white background
- Yellow text on white background
- Red text on green background

**Test it:** If you can't read it from 10 feet away, the contrast is too low.

---

### 5. Don't Rely on Color Alone

**❌ Wrong:**
- "Items in red are high priority"

**✅ Right:**
- "Items marked with ⚠️ are high priority"
- Use color AND another indicator (icon, bold, underline)

**Why:** Colorblind users can't distinguish red from green.

---

### 6. Make Links Descriptive

**❌ Wrong:**
- "Click here"
- "Learn more"

**✅ Right:**
- "View the City Council agenda"
- "Download the Parks Master Plan (PDF)"

---

## Reading Order

**Check the reading order:**

1. Home tab → Arrange → Selection Pane
2. Objects are read from bottom to top
3. Drag to reorder if needed

**Why:** Screen readers read objects in the order they appear in the Selection Pane, not the visual order on the slide.

---

## Tables in PowerPoint

**If you must use a table:**
1. Insert → Table (use the table tools)
2. Right-click table → Table Properties
3. Check "Header Row"

**Better option:** Use a bulleted list instead of a table when possible.

---

## Animations and Transitions

**Use sparingly and with purpose.**

**Avoid:**
- Excessive animations that distract
- Flashing or blinking content (can trigger seizures)
- Auto-advancing slides (users need control)

**If you use animations:**
- Keep them simple
- Ensure content is still understandable without them

---

## Font and Text Best Practices

### Font Choice
**Good fonts:**
- Sans-serif fonts (Arial, Calibri, Verdana)
- Minimum 18pt for body text
- Minimum 24pt for headings

**Avoid:**
- Decorative or script fonts
- All caps (harder to read)
- Italics for long passages

### Text Layout
- Left-align text (not justified)
- Use bullet points, not long paragraphs
- Limit to 6 lines per slide

---

## The Accessibility Checker

**Use it before sharing:**

1. Review tab → Check Accessibility
2. Fix each item in the sidebar
3. Re-check until "No issues found"

**Common issues it catches:**
- Missing alt text
- Low contrast
- Missing slide titles
- Improper reading order

---

## Real-World Example

### ❌ Before (Inaccessible Slide)
- Blank slide with text boxes scattered around
- No slide title
- Image with no alt text
- Light gray text on white background
- "Click here for more info" link

---

### ✅ After (Accessible Slide)

**Slide Title:** "2024 Water Conservation Results"

**Content:**
- Used "Title and Content" layout
- Alt text on chart: "Bar chart showing water usage decreased 15% in 2024"
- Dark blue text on white background
- Link text: "View the full Water Quality Report (PDF)"

---

## Presenting Accessibly

**During the presentation:**

1. **Describe visual content:** "This chart shows a 15% decrease in water usage"
2. **Read text on slides:** Don't just say "As you can see here"
3. **Provide handouts:** Share the deck in advance if possible
4. **Use a microphone:** Even in small rooms, for hearing aid users
5. **Face the audience:** Don't talk to the screen

---

## Sharing Your Presentation

**Best practices:**

1. **Save as PDF with tags:**
   - File → Save As → PDF
   - Options → Check "Document structure tags for accessibility"

2. **Or share the PowerPoint file:**
   - Accessible PowerPoint files work with screen readers
   - Recipients can navigate at their own pace

3. **Post to SharePoint:**
   - Upload to your department's SharePoint library
   - Add a descriptive file name

---

## Quick Accessibility Checklist

Before sharing any PowerPoint:
- [ ] Used built-in slide layouts
- [ ] Every slide has a unique title
- [ ] Added alt text to all images/charts
- [ ] Checked color contrast
- [ ] Made links descriptive
- [ ] Verified reading order
- [ ] Ran Accessibility Checker
- [ ] Fixed all errors

---

## Remember

**An accessible presentation = An inclusive meeting.**

Everyone deserves to participate fully in public meetings and presentations.

---

## Resources

- **Built-in tool:** Review tab → Check Accessibility
- **Microsoft guide:** [Make your PowerPoint presentations accessible](https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/make-your-powerpoint-presentations-accessible-6f7772b2-2f33-4bd2-8ca7-dae3b2b3ef25)

title: Accessible Social Media
content:
# Module 110: Accessible Social Media

**Role:** Communications Staff | **Time:** 6 Minutes | **Difficulty:** Beginner

---

## Why Social Media Accessibility Matters

Social media is how many residents get city updates. If your posts aren't accessible, you're excluding people with disabilities from important community information.

---

## The 4 Essential Rules

### 1. Add Alt Text to Images

**Every platform has this feature:**

**Facebook/Instagram:**
1. Upload image
2. Click "Edit" or "Advanced Settings"
3. Add "Alt Text"

**Twitter/X:**
1. Upload image
2. Click "Add description"
3. Write alt text (up to 1000 characters)

**LinkedIn:**
1. Upload image
2. Click the pencil icon on the image
3. Add "Alt text"

**What to write:**
- Describe what's in the image
- Include text that appears in the image
- Keep it concise but informative

**Example:**
- ❌ Bad: "Image"
- ✅ Good: "Mayor Johnson and Public Works crew filling potholes on Manitou Avenue"

---

### 2. Use CamelCase for Hashtags

**❌ Wrong:**
- #manitousprings
- #communityevent

**✅ Right:**
- #ManitouSprings
- #CommunityEvent

**Why:** Screen readers read #manitousprings as one word ("manitousprings"). CamelCase helps them pronounce it correctly ("Manitou Springs").

---

### 3. Limit Emoji Use

**Do:**
- Use 1-2 emojis for emphasis
- Place them at the END of the post

**Don't:**
- Use emojis in the middle of sentences
- Use multiple emojis in a row
- Replace words with emojis

**Why:** Screen readers read emoji descriptions. "Fire emoji, fire emoji, fire emoji" is annoying.

**Example:**

❌ Wrong:
```
Join us 🎉 for our community 🏘️ cleanup 🧹 event!
```
Screen reader says: "Join us party popper for our community house buildings cleanup broom event"

✅ Right:
```
Join us for our community cleanup event! 🎉
```

---

### 4. Add Captions to Videos

**Every video needs:**
- Captions/subtitles for dialogue and important sounds
- Audio description for visual-only content (if possible)

**How:**

**Facebook/Instagram:**
- Auto-captions available (review for accuracy)
- Or upload SRT caption file

**YouTube:**
- Auto-captions available (ALWAYS review and edit)
- Or upload your own captions

**Twitter/X:**
- Upload video with captions burned in
- Or use Twitter's caption feature

**Caption tips:**
- Include speaker names if multiple people
- Include important sounds: [applause], [siren], [music]
- Fix auto-caption errors (they're often wrong)

---

## Writing Accessible Post Text

### Use Plain Language

**❌ Complex:**
"The municipality will commence infrastructure rehabilitation on Ruxton Avenue."

**✅ Plain:**
"The City will start fixing Ruxton Avenue roads."

---

### Break Up Long Posts

**Do:**
- Use line breaks for readability
- Keep paragraphs short (2-3 sentences)
- Use bullet points when listing items

**Don't:**
- Write one giant paragraph
- Use special characters to create visual spacing

---

### Make Links Descriptive

**❌ Wrong:**
- "Click here: bit.ly/abc123"

**✅ Right:**
- "Read the full story: [link]"
- Or use the platform's link preview feature

---

## Platform-Specific Tips

### Facebook
- Use the "Add Alt Text" feature on all images
- Review auto-captions on videos
- Use Facebook's accessibility checker (in post composer)

### Instagram
- Add alt text before posting (can't edit after)
- Use Stories' text feature for important info (don't rely on images alone)
- Limit hashtags to 3-5 relevant ones

### Twitter/X
- Use the alt text feature (up to 1000 characters)
- Thread long announcements instead of posting as an image
- Enable image descriptions in settings

### LinkedIn
- Add alt text to all images
- Use native video captions
- Keep posts professional and concise

---

## Graphics and Infographics

**If you post an infographic:**

1. **Add comprehensive alt text** describing the key information
2. **Or better:** Post the information as text in the caption
3. **Best:** Link to an accessible web page with the full information

**Why:** Infographics are often unreadable on mobile and inaccessible to screen readers.

**Example:**

❌ Wrong:
- Post infographic with no alt text or caption

✅ Right:
```
[Image: Infographic showing water conservation tips]

Alt text: "5 water conservation tips: 1. Fix leaky faucets, 2. Water plants in the morning, 3. Use a broom instead of hose, 4. Take shorter showers, 5. Run full loads of laundry"

Caption: "Save water this summer! 💧
• Fix leaky faucets
• Water plants in the morning
• Use a broom instead of hose
• Take shorter showers
• Run full loads of laundry

Learn more: [link to city website]"
```

---

## Live Events and Stories

**When posting live events:**

1. **Describe what's happening** in the text, not just in the video
2. **Use captions** if speaking
3. **Provide context** for visual-only content

**Instagram/Facebook Stories:**
- Use the text feature for important information
- Don't rely on images alone
- Add captions to video stories

---

## Accessibility Checklist for Social Posts

Before posting:
- [ ] Added alt text to all images
- [ ] Used CamelCase for hashtags
- [ ] Limited emoji use (1-2 at the end)
- [ ] Added captions to videos
- [ ] Used plain language
- [ ] Made links descriptive
- [ ] Broke up long text with line breaks
- [ ] Reviewed auto-captions for accuracy

---

## Real-World Example

### ❌ Before (Inaccessible Post)

```
🔥🔥🔥 BIG NEWS 🔥🔥🔥
Check this out 👇
[Image of event flyer with no alt text]
#manitousprings #event #fun #community #colorado
```

**Problems:**
- Excessive emojis
- No alt text on image
- "Check this out" is not descriptive
- Lowercase hashtags
- Information only in image (not accessible)

---

### ✅ After (Accessible Post)

```
Join us for the Annual Manitou Springs Art Fair!

📅 Saturday, June 15, 10am-5pm
📍 Memorial Park
🎨 50+ local artists, live music, food trucks

Free admission. All ages welcome.

Register your booth: [link]

[Image: Colorful banner reading "Manitou Springs Art Fair - June 15"]

Alt text: "Colorful banner with paint splashes reading 'Manitou Springs Art Fair, June 15, Memorial Park, 10am-5pm'"

#ManitouSprings #ArtFair #CommunityEvent 🎨
```

**Fixed:**
- Clear, descriptive text
- All event info in the post text (not just the image)
- Alt text on image
- CamelCase hashtags
- One emoji at the end
- Descriptive link

---

## Remember

**Accessible social media = Inclusive communication.**

Everyone deserves to know what's happening in their community.

---

## Resources

- **Facebook Accessibility:** [Facebook's accessibility guide](https://www.facebook.com/help/273947702950567)
- **Twitter/X Accessibility:** Enable image descriptions in Settings
- **Instagram Accessibility:** [Instagram's accessibility features](https://help.instagram.com/503708446705527)

title: Web Content Accessibility Basics
content:
# Module 111: Web Content Accessibility Basics

**Role:** Web Editors | **Time:** 10 Minutes | **Difficulty:** Intermediate

---

## Why Web Accessibility Matters

Your city website is a public service. If it's not accessible, you're denying services to residents with disabilities—and violating federal law (ADA Title II).

---

## The POUR Principles

All web content must be:

### **P**erceivable
Users can perceive the information (it's not invisible to any of their senses)

### **O**perable
Users can operate the interface (they can use it with keyboard, mouse, or assistive tech)

### **U**nderstandable
Users can understand the information and how to use the interface

### **R**obust
Content works with current and future technologies (including assistive tech)

---

## The 10 Essential Rules

### 1. Every Image Needs Alt Text

**How in SharePoint:**
1. Click the image
2. Click the pencil icon (Edit)
3. Add "Alternative text"

**What to write:**
- **Informative images:** Describe the information
- **Decorative images:** Leave alt text empty or mark as decorative
- **Linked images:** Describe the destination

---

### 2. Use Proper Heading Structure

**The rule:**
- One H1 per page (the page title)
- H2 for main sections
- H3 for subsections under H2
- Never skip levels (don't go H1 → H3)

**How in SharePoint:**
1. Highlight text
2. Use the Format dropdown
3. Select Heading 1, 2, or 3

**Why:** Screen readers navigate by headings. Proper structure = easy navigation.

---

### 3. Make Links Descriptive

**❌ Wrong:**
- "Click here"
- "Read more"
- "Learn more"

**✅ Right:**
- "Read the 2024 Budget Summary"
- "Download the Parks Master Plan (PDF, 2MB)"
- "Contact the Public Works Department"

**The test:** If you read just the link text, would you know where it goes?

---

### 4. Ensure Sufficient Color Contrast

**Minimum ratios:**
- Normal text: 4.5:1
- Large text (18pt+): 3:1

**Good combinations:**
- Black on white
- Dark blue on white
- White on dark blue

**Bad combinations:**
- Light gray on white
- Yellow on white
- Red on green

**Test it:** Use the [WebAIM Contrast Checker](https://webaim.org/resources/contrastchecker/)

---

### 5. Don't Use Color Alone to Convey Information

**❌ Wrong:**
- "Required fields are in red"
- "Click the green button to continue"

**✅ Right:**
- "Required fields are marked with an asterisk (*)"
- "Click the 'Continue' button to proceed"

**Why:** Colorblind users can't distinguish colors.

---

### 6. Make All Functionality Keyboard Accessible

**The test:**
1. Unplug your mouse
2. Use only Tab, Enter, and arrow keys
3. Can you access everything?

**Common issues:**
- Dropdown menus that only work with mouse hover
- Custom buttons that don't respond to Enter key
- Forms that can't be completed with keyboard

**Fix:** Use standard SharePoint web parts (they're keyboard accessible by default)

---

### 7. Provide Text Alternatives for Video/Audio

**Every video needs:**
- Captions for dialogue and important sounds
- Transcript (text version of audio content)

**Every audio-only file needs:**
- Transcript

**How in SharePoint:**
- Upload video with captions to Stream
- Add transcript as a text web part below the video

---

### 8. Use Tables for Data, Not Layout

**Use tables for:**
- Data with rows and columns
- Information that needs headers

**Don't use tables for:**
- Page layout (use SharePoint sections instead)
- Side-by-side content (use columns)

**If you must use a table:**
1. Use the Table web part
2. Designate header rows
3. Keep it simple (avoid merged cells)

---

### 9. Write in Plain Language

**Do:**
- Use short sentences
- Use common words
- Define technical terms
- Use active voice

**Don't:**
- Use jargon or bureaucratic language
- Write long, complex sentences
- Assume everyone knows city acronyms

**Example:**

❌ Complex:
"The municipality will commence infrastructure rehabilitation on Ruxton Avenue, necessitating temporary traffic pattern modifications."

✅ Plain:
"The City will start fixing Ruxton Avenue roads. Traffic will be detoured during construction."

---

### 10. Provide Clear Error Messages

**❌ Wrong:**
- "Error 404"
- "Invalid input"
- "Submission failed"

**✅ Right:**
- "Page not found. Try searching for what you need."
- "Please enter a valid email address (example: name@email.com)"
- "Your form wasn't submitted. Please fill in all required fields marked with *"

---

## SharePoint-Specific Tips

### Use Built-In Web Parts
SharePoint's standard web parts are accessible. Custom code may not be.

**Accessible web parts:**
- Text
- Image
- Hero
- Quick Links
- News
- Document Library
- List

---

### Check Page Accessibility

**Before publishing:**
1. Click the page title
2. Look for the accessibility icon
3. Fix any issues shown

---

### Use the Accessibility Checker

**In SharePoint:**
1. Edit the page
2. The accessibility checker runs automatically
3. Fix errors before publishing

---

## Common Web Accessibility Errors

### ❌ Error 1: Missing Alt Text
**Problem:** Images with no alt text

**Fix:** Add alt text to every image

---

### ❌ Error 2: Low Contrast
**Problem:** Text is hard to read

**Fix:** Use darker text or lighter background

---

### ❌ Error 3: Empty Links
**Problem:** Links with no text (just an icon)

**Fix:** Add descriptive text or aria-label

---

### ❌ Error 4: Skipped Heading Levels
**Problem:** Going from H1 to H3

**Fix:** Use proper heading hierarchy (H1 → H2 → H3)

---

### ❌ Error 5: PDF Without Tags
**Problem:** Posting an inaccessible PDF

**Fix:** Create accessible PDFs (see Module 107) or post as web page instead

---

## Testing Your Page

### Manual Tests

1. **Keyboard test:** Navigate with Tab key only
2. **Zoom test:** Zoom to 200% - is everything still readable?
3. **Screen reader test:** Use NVDA (free) or JAWS to test
4. **Color blind test:** Use a color blindness simulator

### Automated Tests

**Free tools:**
- WAVE browser extension
- axe DevTools browser extension
- SharePoint's built-in checker

**Remember:** Automated tools catch ~30% of issues. Manual testing is essential.

---

## Real-World Example

### ❌ Before (Inaccessible Page)

```
[Large image with text, no alt text]

Click here for more info.

Important dates:
2/15 - Meeting
3/3 - Deadline
```

**Problems:**
- Image has no alt text
- "Click here" link
- Dates have no context
- No headings

---

### ✅ After (Accessible Page)

```
Community Budget Workshop (Heading 1)

Join us to discuss the 2024 city budget and share your priorities.

[Image: City Council members reviewing budget documents]
Alt text: "City Council members reviewing budget documents at community workshop"

Important Dates (Heading 2)

• February 15 - Community Budget Workshop, 6pm at City Hall
• March 3 - Budget comment deadline

Register for the workshop or submit written comments.
```

**Fixed:**
- Proper heading structure
- Alt text on image
- Descriptive link text
- Full context for dates

---

## Accessibility is Not Optional

**Legal requirements:**
- ADA Title II (government services)
- Section 508 (federal funding)
- WCAG 2.1 Level AA (industry standard)

**Consequences of non-compliance:**
- Lawsuits
- OCR complaints
- Denied services to residents
- Reputational damage

---

## Quick Checklist

Before publishing any web page:
- [ ] All images have alt text
- [ ] Proper heading structure (H1, H2, H3)
- [ ] Links are descriptive
- [ ] Color contrast is sufficient
- [ ] Not relying on color alone
- [ ] Keyboard accessible
- [ ] Videos have captions
- [ ] Plain language used
- [ ] Ran accessibility checker
- [ ] Fixed all errors

---

## Remember

**Accessible web content = Equal access to city services.**

It's not just the law—it's the right thing to do.

---

## Resources

- **WCAG Guidelines:** [Web Content Accessibility Guidelines](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/quickref/)
- **WebAIM:** [WebAIM.org](https://webaim.org/) - Excellent tutorials and tools
- **Free screen reader:** [NVDA](https://www.nvaccess.org/download/)
- **Contrast checker:** [WebAIM Contrast Checker](https://webaim.org/resources/contrastchecker/)

title: Accessible Forms and Surveys
content:
# Module 112: Accessible Forms and Surveys

**Role:** All Staff | **Time:** 8 Minutes | **Difficulty:** Intermediate

---

## Why Form Accessibility Matters

Forms are how residents interact with city services—permits, registrations, feedback, and more. An inaccessible form means some residents can't access services at all.

---

## The 7 Essential Rules

### 1. Every Field Needs a Clear Label

**❌ Wrong:**
- Placeholder text only ("Enter your name here...")
- No label at all
- Label not associated with the field

**✅ Right:**
- Visible label above or beside the field
- Label stays visible when field is filled
- Label is programmatically associated with the field

**Why:** Placeholder text disappears when you start typing. Screen readers need permanent labels.

---

### 2. Mark Required Fields Clearly

**❌ Wrong:**
- Using color alone (red border)
- Using asterisk with no explanation
- No indication at all

**✅ Right:**
- Add "(required)" to the label
- Or use asterisk (*) with explanation at top: "Fields marked with * are required"
- Use both visual and text indicators

**Example:**
```
Email Address (required)
[text field]

OR

* Email Address
[text field]

Note at top of form: "Fields marked with * are required"
```

---

### 3. Group Related Fields

**Use fieldsets for:**
- Radio button groups
- Checkbox groups
- Related information (address fields, contact info)

**How in Microsoft Forms:**
- Forms automatically groups radio buttons and checkboxes
- Use section headers to group related questions

**How in SharePoint forms:**
- Use the "Section" feature
- Add clear headings for each group

---

### 4. Provide Clear Instructions

**Do:**
- Explain format requirements upfront
- Give examples
- Place instructions before the field, not after

**Example:**

❌ Wrong:
```
Phone Number: [_______]
Format: 555-123-4567
```

✅ Right:
```
Phone Number (format: 555-123-4567)
[_______]
```

---

### 5. Use Appropriate Input Types

**Choose the right field type:**
- **Text:** Names, addresses
- **Email:** Email addresses (validates format)
- **Number:** Quantities, ages
- **Date:** Dates (provides date picker)
- **Radio buttons:** Choose one option
- **Checkboxes:** Choose multiple options
- **Dropdown:** Many options (use sparingly)

**Why:** Correct input types help mobile users (shows right keyboard) and assistive tech users.

---

### 6. Provide Helpful Error Messages

**❌ Wrong:**
- "Error"
- "Invalid input"
- Red border with no explanation

**✅ Right:**
- "Please enter a valid email address (example: name@email.com)"
- "Phone number must be 10 digits"
- "This field is required"

**Best practices:**
- Explain what's wrong
- Explain how to fix it
- Show errors near the field (not just at top)
- Don't clear the form on error

---

### 7. Make Forms Keyboard Accessible

**The test:**
1. Use only Tab, Shift+Tab, Enter, and arrow keys
2. Can you fill out the entire form?
3. Can you submit it?

**Common issues:**
- Can't reach all fields with Tab
- Can't select radio buttons with arrow keys
- Submit button doesn't work with Enter key

**Fix:** Use standard form controls (they're keyboard accessible by default)

---

## Microsoft Forms Best Practices

### Question Types

**Use:**
- **Choice (single answer):** For radio button questions
- **Choice (multiple answers):** For checkbox questions
- **Text:** For short answers
- **Long answer:** For paragraphs
- **Rating:** For satisfaction scales
- **Date:** For date selection

---

### Settings to Enable

**Before sharing your form:**

1. **Settings** → **Options**
2. ✅ Enable "One response per person" (if appropriate)
3. ✅ Enable "Record name" (if you need to know who responded)
4. ✅ Enable "Customized thank you message"

---

### Accessibility Features in Forms

**Microsoft Forms is accessible by default, but:**

1. **Write clear questions:** Use plain language
2. **Add descriptions:** Use the subtitle field for instructions
3. **Use sections:** Group related questions
4. **Test it:** Fill it out with keyboard only

---

## SharePoint List Forms

**When creating custom forms:**

1. **Use the default form** when possible (it's accessible)
2. **If customizing with Power Apps:**
   - Add labels to all fields
   - Set tab order logically
   - Test with keyboard
   - Test with screen reader

---

## Common Form Accessibility Errors

### ❌ Error 1: Placeholder Text as Label

**Problem:**
```
[Enter your email address...]
```

**Fix:**
```
Email Address
[_____________]
```

---

### ❌ Error 2: Unclear Required Fields

**Problem:** Red border with no explanation

**Fix:** Add "(required)" to label or use asterisk with legend

---

### ❌ Error 3: CAPTCHA Without Alternative

**Problem:** "Click all images with traffic lights" (impossible for blind users)

**Fix:** Use reCAPTCHA v3 (invisible) or provide audio alternative

---

### ❌ Error 4: Time Limits

**Problem:** Form times out before user can complete it

**Fix:** 
- No time limits
- Or warn user and allow extension
- Or save progress automatically

---

### ❌ Error 5: Confusing Error Messages

**Problem:** "Error: Field 3 invalid"

**Fix:** "Please enter your phone number in this format: 555-123-4567"

---

## Real-World Example

### ❌ Before (Inaccessible Form)

```
Name: [_______]
[_______] (no label for last name field)

Email [_______]

Phone [_______]

What's your favorite park?
○ Memorial
○ Soda Springs
○ Schryver

[Submit]
```

**Problems:**
- Last name field has no label
- No indication of required fields
- No format instruction for phone
- Submit button might not be keyboard accessible

---

### ✅ After (Accessible Form)

```
Park Feedback Form

Fields marked with * are required.

* First Name
[_____________]

* Last Name
[_____________]

* Email Address
[_____________]

Phone Number (optional, format: 555-123-4567)
[_____________]

* What's your favorite Manitou Springs park?
○ Memorial Park
○ Soda Springs Park
○ Schryver Park
○ Other

[Submit Feedback]
```

**Fixed:**
- All fields have clear labels
- Required fields marked with *
- Legend explains asterisk
- Phone format specified
- Descriptive submit button

---

## Multi-Page Forms

**If your form has multiple pages:**

1. **Show progress:** "Step 2 of 4"
2. **Allow going back:** Don't lose data
3. **Save progress:** For long forms
4. **Clear navigation:** "Next" and "Previous" buttons

---

## Form Confirmation

**After submission:**

1. **Show clear confirmation:** "Your form has been submitted"
2. **Provide confirmation number:** If applicable
3. **Explain next steps:** "We'll respond within 2 business days"
4. **Send confirmation email:** With copy of submission

---

## Testing Your Form

### Manual Tests

1. **Keyboard test:** Fill out with Tab and Enter only
2. **Screen reader test:** Use NVDA or JAWS
3. **Mobile test:** Complete on phone
4. **Error test:** Submit with errors - are messages clear?
5. **Zoom test:** Zoom to 200% - still usable?

### Test with Real Users

- Ask a colleague to complete it
- Watch for confusion or difficulty
- Ask for feedback

---

## Quick Checklist

Before publishing any form:
- [ ] All fields have clear, visible labels
- [ ] Required fields are marked clearly
- [ ] Instructions are provided before fields
- [ ] Appropriate input types used
- [ ] Error messages are helpful and specific
- [ ] Form is keyboard accessible
- [ ] Tested with Tab key only
- [ ] Confirmation message is clear
- [ ] Mobile-friendly

---

## Remember

**Accessible forms = Equal access to city services.**

If residents can't fill out your form, they can't access the service.

---

## Resources

- **Microsoft Forms:** Built-in accessibility features
- **Form accessibility guide:** [WebAIM Forms Guide](https://webaim.org/techniques/forms/)
- **Free screen reader:** [NVDA](https://www.nvaccess.org/download/)

title: Accessible Meetings and Events
content:
# Module 113: Accessible Meetings and Events

**Role:** All Staff | **Time:** 7 Minutes | **Difficulty:** Beginner

---

## Why Meeting Accessibility Matters

Public meetings are required by law to be accessible. But beyond compliance, accessible meetings ensure all residents can participate in their local government.

---

## Before the Meeting: Planning

### 1. Choose an Accessible Venue

**Check for:**
- ✅ Wheelchair-accessible entrance and parking
- ✅ Accessible restrooms
- ✅ Clear pathways (no obstacles)
- ✅ Good lighting
- ✅ Minimal background noise

**For virtual meetings:**
- Use platforms with captions (Zoom, Teams)
- Test technology in advance

---

### 2. Provide Advance Notice

**In your meeting announcement:**
- Date, time, and location
- Accessibility features available
- How to request accommodations
- Contact person for questions

**Example:**
```
City Council Meeting
Tuesday, March 5, 6:00 PM
City Hall Council Chambers

Accessible parking and entrance available.
ASL interpretation and CART captioning provided.

For other accommodations, contact:
Jane Smith, 555-1234, jsmith@manitousprings.gov
Requests should be made 5 business days in advance.
```

---

### 3. Prepare Accessible Materials

**All handouts and presentations must be accessible:**
- Use proper headings in Word docs
- Add alt text to images in PowerPoint
- Provide materials in advance when possible
- Offer large print or electronic versions

---

## During the Meeting: Facilitation

### 1. Use a Microphone

**Always use a microphone, even in small rooms.**

**Why:**
- Hearing aid users need the audio signal
- People in the back can't hear
- Virtual participants need clear audio
- Recording quality improves

---

### 2. Identify Yourself When Speaking

**Do this:**
"This is John Smith from Public Works. I'd like to add..."

**Why:**
- Blind attendees can't see who's speaking
- Virtual participants may not see video
- Creates clear meeting record

---

### 3. Describe Visual Content

**When showing slides, charts, or videos:**
- Describe what's on the screen
- Read text on slides aloud
- Explain charts and graphs verbally

**Example:**
❌ Wrong: "As you can see here..."
✅ Right: "This bar chart shows water usage decreased 15% in 2024."

---

### 4. Provide Real-Time Captions

**Options:**
- **CART (Communication Access Realtime Translation):** Professional captioner
- **ASL Interpreter:** For Deaf community members
- **Automated captions:** Zoom, Teams (review for accuracy)

**Best practice:** Offer both CART and ASL when possible

---

### 5. Allow Multiple Ways to Participate

**Offer:**
- In-person attendance
- Virtual attendance (Zoom/Teams)
- Written comments (email, online form)
- Phone participation

**Why:** Not everyone can attend in person or speak publicly

---

## Virtual Meeting Best Practices

### Platform Features to Enable

**In Zoom/Teams:**
- ✅ Live captions
- ✅ Chat for questions
- ✅ Screen reader compatibility
- ✅ Keyboard shortcuts
- ✅ Recording with captions

---

### Meeting Etiquette

**Do:**
- Mute when not speaking
- Use "Raise Hand" feature
- State your name before speaking
- Describe what you're showing on screen
- Monitor chat for questions

**Don't:**
- Talk over others
- Share screen without describing content
- Ignore chat questions
- Use visual-only cues ("raise your hand if...")

---

## Accessible Event Planning

### Registration

**Make registration forms accessible:**
- Clear labels on all fields
- Option to request accommodations
- Confirmation email with details

**Ask about accommodations:**
"Do you need any accommodations to participate? (ASL interpreter, wheelchair seating, dietary restrictions, etc.)"

---

### Signage

**At the event:**
- Large, high-contrast signs
- Clear directional arrows
- Accessible parking signs
- Restroom signs with symbols

---

### Seating

**Provide:**
- Wheelchair-accessible seating (not just in back)
- Companion seating next to wheelchair spaces
- Reserved seating for people with disabilities
- Clear sightlines to interpreter/screen

---

## Common Accessibility Accommodations

### ASL Interpretation
**When:** Requested by Deaf attendees
**How:** Hire certified interpreter through agency
**Cost:** Budget for this in all public meetings

### CART Captioning
**When:** Requested or for all large meetings
**How:** Hire CART provider
**Display:** Project on screen or provide individual monitor

### Assistive Listening Devices
**When:** For people with hearing loss
**How:** Rent or purchase FM system or hearing loop

### Large Print Materials
**When:** Requested by people with low vision
**How:** Print handouts in 18pt+ font

### Service Animals
**Always allowed.** Don't ask questions or restrict access.

---

## Public Comment Accessibility

**Ensure everyone can participate:**

1. **Announce how to comment:**
   - "To speak, raise your hand or press *9 on phone"
   - "To submit written comment, use the form at..."

2. **Allow adequate time:**
   - Don't rush speakers
   - Allow extra time for people using interpreters or assistive tech

3. **Repeat questions:**
   - If someone asks a question, repeat it for virtual attendees

4. **Provide alternatives:**
   - Written comments
   - Video submissions
   - Email

---

## Real-World Example

### ❌ Before (Inaccessible Meeting)

- No microphone ("It's a small room")
- PowerPoint with no verbal description
- No captions for virtual attendees
- "Raise your hand if you agree" (visual-only)
- No way to submit questions remotely

---

### ✅ After (Accessible Meeting)

- Microphone used by all speakers
- "This slide shows our three priorities: safety, sustainability, and community engagement"
- Live captions enabled in Zoom
- "Please use the raise hand feature or type 'agree' in chat"
- Chat monitored for questions, read aloud by facilitator

---

## Emergency Accessibility

**If someone requests an accommodation you can't provide:**

1. **Don't say no.** Say "Let me find out how we can make that work"
2. **Contact your ADA coordinator** or HR
3. **Provide alternative** if immediate accommodation isn't possible
4. **Follow up** to ensure future meetings are accessible

**Remember:** Denying reasonable accommodations violates the ADA.

---

## Post-Meeting: Follow-Up

### 1. Share Accessible Materials

**After the meeting:**
- Post accessible minutes
- Share presentation (with alt text)
- Provide recording with captions
- Publish transcript

---

### 2. Solicit Feedback

**Ask:**
"Were you able to fully participate in today's meeting? What could we improve?"

---

## Quick Checklist

Before any public meeting or event:
- [ ] Venue is accessible (parking, entrance, restrooms)
- [ ] Accommodations notice in announcement
- [ ] Materials prepared in accessible format
- [ ] Microphone available and tested
- [ ] Captions enabled for virtual attendees
- [ ] Multiple participation methods offered
- [ ] Staff trained on accessibility procedures
- [ ] Contact person designated for accommodation requests

---

## Remember

**Accessible meetings = Inclusive democracy.**

Everyone has the right to participate in their local government.

---

## Resources

- **ADA requirements:** [ADA Title II](https://www.ada.gov/topics/title-ii/)
- **CART providers:** Search "CART services Colorado"
- **ASL interpreters:** [Registry of Interpreters for the Deaf](https://rid.org/)
- **Zoom accessibility:** [Zoom Accessibility Features](https://zoom.us/accessibility)

title: Accessible Customer Service
content:
# Module 114: Accessible Customer Service

**Role:** Front-Line Staff | **Time:** 8 Minutes | **Difficulty:** Beginner

---

## Why Accessible Customer Service Matters

You are often the first point of contact for residents. How you interact with people with disabilities sets the tone for their entire experience with the city.

---

## General Principles

### 1. Talk Directly to the Person

**Do:**
- Make eye contact with the person, not their companion or interpreter
- Address them directly: "How can I help you?"

**Don't:**
- Talk to their companion: "What does he need?"
- Talk to their interpreter
- Raise your voice unless asked

---

### 2. Ask Before Helping

**Do:**
- "Would you like assistance?"
- Wait for their response
- Follow their instructions

**Don't:**
- Grab a wheelchair without asking
- Pet or distract a service animal
- Assume they need help

---

### 3. Be Patient

**Do:**
- Allow extra time for communication
- Listen carefully
- Ask for clarification if needed

**Don't:**
- Rush the conversation
- Finish their sentences
- Act frustrated

---

## Specific Disabilities

### Working with Blind or Low Vision Customers

**Do:**
- Identify yourself: "Hi, I'm Sarah from the front desk"
- Offer your arm if guiding: "Would you like to take my arm?"
- Describe the environment: "The counter is to your right"
- Read forms or documents aloud if requested
- Provide information in accessible format (large print, email, audio)

**Don't:**
- Leave without saying you're leaving
- Move their belongings without telling them
- Pet or distract their service dog
- Point or use visual-only directions

**Example:**
❌ Wrong: "The form is over there" (pointing)
✅ Right: "The form is on the counter to your right, about 3 feet away"

---

### Working with Deaf or Hard of Hearing Customers

**Do:**
- Face them directly (they may read lips)
- Speak clearly at normal volume
- Use written communication if needed
- Provide a pen and paper
- Use video relay service if available
- Book an ASL interpreter for complex transactions

**Don't:**
- Cover your mouth when speaking
- Shout (it doesn't help)
- Exaggerate lip movements
- Turn away while speaking

**Communication options:**
- Writing back and forth
- Typing on computer
- Video relay service (VRS)
- ASL interpreter (for complex matters)

---

### Working with Customers with Mobility Disabilities

**Do:**
- Sit or crouch to be at eye level if they're in a wheelchair
- Move obstacles if they're blocking access
- Hold doors open
- Offer to carry items if asked

**Don't:**
- Lean on their wheelchair (it's part of their personal space)
- Move their wheelchair without permission
- Pat them on the head
- Park in accessible parking spaces (even "just for a minute")

**Accessible counter service:**
- Have a lowered counter section
- Come around the counter to assist if needed
- Bring forms to them

---

### Working with Customers with Speech Disabilities

**Do:**
- Listen patiently
- Ask them to repeat if you don't understand
- Repeat back what you understood to confirm
- Offer written communication if helpful
- Give them time to finish

**Don't:**
- Pretend you understand if you don't
- Finish their sentences
- Speak for them
- Act impatient

**Example:**
"I want to make sure I understand correctly. You need to [repeat what you understood]. Is that right?"

---

### Working with Customers with Cognitive or Developmental Disabilities

**Do:**
- Use plain language
- Break information into steps
- Be patient and supportive
- Provide written instructions
- Repeat information if needed

**Don't:**
- Use baby talk
- Be condescending
- Rush them
- Assume they can't understand

**Example:**
❌ Wrong: "You need to submit the application pursuant to municipal code section 12.3.4"
✅ Right: "Here's what you need to do: 1. Fill out this form. 2. Bring it back to me. 3. We'll process it within 5 days."

---

## Service Animals

### The Rules

**Service animals are:**
- Allowed everywhere the public is allowed
- Working animals, not pets
- Usually dogs (sometimes miniature horses)

**You may ask:**
1. "Is this a service animal?"
2. "What task is it trained to perform?"

**You may NOT ask:**
- "What's your disability?"
- "Can you prove it's a service animal?"
- For documentation or certification

**You may NOT:**
- Pet the animal (it's working)
- Feed the animal
- Distract the animal
- Deny access because of the animal

**Exception:** You can ask them to remove the animal if:
- It's out of control and the handler doesn't control it
- It's not housebroken

---

## Phone Etiquette

### General Tips

**Do:**
- Speak clearly at normal pace
- Identify yourself and your department
- Ask how you can help
- Offer to call back if you need to research something

**Don't:**
- Put them on hold without asking
- Transfer without explaining why
- Use speakerphone without asking

---

### Relay Calls

**What is it:**
A relay operator facilitates calls for people who are Deaf or hard of hearing.

**How it works:**
- You'll hear the operator say "This is a relay call"
- Speak directly to the caller, not the operator
- The operator will relay everything you say
- There may be a slight delay

**Do:**
- Speak directly: "How can I help you?" (not "Tell them...")
- Be patient with delays
- Treat it like any other call

---

## Counter and Lobby Accessibility

### Physical Space

**Ensure:**
- Clear pathways (no boxes or obstacles)
- Lowered counter section
- Accessible seating in waiting area
- Good lighting
- Clear signage

---

### Materials

**Provide:**
- Large print forms (18pt+)
- Forms available electronically
- Pens that work
- Clipboards for standing customers

---

### Technology

**Offer:**
- Assistive listening devices
- TTY or video relay service
- Accessible website for online services
- Email communication option

---

## Common Scenarios

### Scenario 1: Customer Using a Wheelchair Can't Reach Counter

**❌ Wrong response:**
"Sorry, that's just how the counter is built"

**✅ Right response:**
"Let me come around to assist you" or "Let me bring the form to you"

---

### Scenario 2: Customer with Service Dog

**❌ Wrong response:**
"Sorry, no dogs allowed"

**✅ Right response:**
Welcome them normally. Don't comment on or interact with the service dog.

---

### Scenario 3: You Don't Understand Customer's Speech

**❌ Wrong response:**
Nod and pretend you understand

**✅ Right response:**
"I want to make sure I help you correctly. Would it be easier to write it down?"

---

### Scenario 4: Blind Customer Needs to Sign Form

**❌ Wrong response:**
"Just sign here" (pointing)

**✅ Right response:**
"I'll guide your hand to the signature line" or "I can read the form to you first, then guide you to the signature line"

---

## Disability Etiquette: Words Matter

### ✅ Use Person-First Language

**Say:**
- Person with a disability
- Person who uses a wheelchair
- Person who is blind
- Person who is Deaf

**Not:**
- Disabled person
- Wheelchair-bound
- The blind
- Hearing impaired

**Exception:** Some people prefer identity-first language ("Deaf person," "autistic person"). Follow their lead.

---

### ❌ Avoid These Terms

**Don't say:**
- Handicapped
- Crippled
- Wheelchair-bound
- Suffers from
- Victim of
- Special needs
- Differently abled

---

## Emergency Procedures

**If there's an emergency:**

1. **Don't leave anyone behind**
2. **Ask how you can help** them evacuate
3. **Assign someone** to assist if needed
4. **Know your building's** accessible evacuation routes
5. **Practice** evacuation procedures that include people with disabilities

---

## Quick Checklist

For every customer interaction:
- [ ] Speak directly to the person
- [ ] Use respectful language
- [ ] Ask before helping
- [ ] Be patient
- [ ] Provide information in accessible format
- [ ] Respect service animals
- [ ] Ensure physical space is accessible
- [ ] Offer alternative communication methods if needed

---

## Remember

**Accessible customer service = Respectful, dignified service for everyone.**

People with disabilities are customers, residents, and taxpayers who deserve the same quality service as everyone else.

---

## Resources

- **ADA National Network:** [adata.org](https://adata.org/) - Free ADA information
- **Disability etiquette:** [United Spinal Association Guide](https://www.unitedspinal.org/disability-etiquette/)
- **Service animal rules:** [ADA Service Animal Requirements](https://www.ada.gov/topics/service-animals/)

title: Legal Requirements and Compliance
content:
# Module 115: Legal Requirements and Compliance

**Role:** All Staff | **Time:** 10 Minutes | **Difficulty:** Intermediate

---

## Why Legal Compliance Matters

Accessibility isn't optional—it's the law. Understanding your legal obligations protects both residents' rights and the city from liability.

---

## The Laws That Apply to Us

### 1. Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) - Title II

**What it covers:**
- All programs, services, and activities of state and local governments
- Physical accessibility of buildings
- Effective communication
- Website and digital accessibility

**Applies to:**
- City Hall and all public buildings
- Public meetings and events
- City website and online services
- Documents and communications
- Employment

**Key requirement:**
"No qualified individual with a disability shall be excluded from participation in or denied the benefits of services, programs, or activities of a public entity."

---

### 2. Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act

**What it covers:**
- Electronic and information technology
- Applies when federal funding is involved

**Applies to:**
- City website
- Software and applications
- Documents (PDFs, Word, etc.)
- Videos and multimedia
- Electronic forms

**Standard:** WCAG 2.0 Level AA (or newer)

---

### 3. Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG)

**Not a law, but the standard:**
- WCAG 2.1 Level AA is the industry standard
- Referenced in ADA lawsuits
- Best practice for all digital content

**The four principles (POUR):**
- **Perceivable:** Users can perceive the information
- **Operable:** Users can operate the interface
- **Understandable:** Users can understand the information
- **Robust:** Content works with assistive technologies

---

## What "Reasonable Accommodation" Means

### Definition
A modification or adjustment that enables a person with a disability to access services, programs, or employment.

### Examples

**For residents:**
- ASL interpreter at public meeting
- Documents in large print or Braille
- Accessible website
- Service animal access
- Accessible parking

**For employees:**
- Assistive technology (screen reader, ergonomic keyboard)
- Modified work schedule
- Accessible workspace
- Sign language interpreter for meetings

---

### When Can You Deny an Accommodation?

**Only if:**
1. **Undue burden:** Would cause significant difficulty or expense (very high bar)
2. **Fundamental alteration:** Would fundamentally change the nature of the service
3. **Direct threat:** Would pose a direct threat to health or safety

**Important:** You can't deny an accommodation just because:
- It's inconvenient
- It costs money (unless truly an undue burden)
- You've never done it before
- You don't think they "look" disabled

**If you can't provide the requested accommodation:**
- Offer an alternative that would be effective
- Document why the original request can't be met
- Consult with your ADA coordinator

---

## Consequences of Non-Compliance

### Legal Consequences

**OCR Complaints:**
- Office for Civil Rights investigates
- Can require corrective action
- Can withhold federal funding

**Lawsuits:**
- Private individuals can sue
- Class action lawsuits possible
- Can result in:
  - Court-ordered changes
  - Monetary damages
  - Attorney fees
  - Ongoing monitoring

**Recent examples:**
- Hundreds of cities sued for inaccessible websites
- Settlements often $50,000-$100,000+
- Required to remediate all digital content

---

### Reputational Consequences

**Beyond legal liability:**
- Negative press coverage
- Loss of public trust
- Damage to city's reputation
- Exclusion of residents from civic life

---

## Your Responsibilities

### As a City Employee

**You must:**
1. **Provide equal access** to all services and programs
2. **Make reasonable accommodations** when requested
3. **Create accessible content** (documents, web pages, social media)
4. **Ensure meetings are accessible** (captions, interpreters, accessible venue)
5. **Respect service animals** and allow access
6. **Communicate effectively** with people with disabilities
7. **Report accessibility barriers** to your supervisor or ADA coordinator

**You must NOT:**
- Deny service because of a disability
- Charge extra for accommodations
- Require proof of disability (except for specific programs)
- Segregate people with disabilities
- Retaliate against someone who requests accommodation

---

## Common Compliance Mistakes

### ❌ Mistake 1: "We'll Make It Accessible If Someone Asks"

**Wrong approach:**
Waiting for complaints before making things accessible.

**Right approach:**
Proactively ensure accessibility from the start.

**Why:**
- People shouldn't have to ask for basic access
- Creates barriers and delays
- Violates the law

---

### ❌ Mistake 2: "It's Too Expensive"

**Wrong approach:**
Denying accommodation because of cost without analysis.

**Right approach:**
- Evaluate actual cost
- Consider alternatives
- Document undue burden (if truly applicable)
- Consult ADA coordinator

**Reality:**
Most accommodations cost little or nothing:
- Alt text: Free
- Captions: Often automated or low-cost
- Accessible documents: Free (just requires training)

---

### ❌ Mistake 3: "Our Website Is Exempt"

**Wrong approach:**
Assuming government websites don't need to be accessible.

**Right approach:**
All government websites must be accessible under ADA Title II.

**Reality:**
- No exemption for small cities
- No exemption for old websites
- No exemption for third-party content

---

### ❌ Mistake 4: "They Don't Look Disabled"

**Wrong approach:**
Questioning whether someone has a disability.

**Right approach:**
- Don't ask about the nature of the disability
- Provide the accommodation
- Respect privacy

**Remember:**
- Not all disabilities are visible
- You're not entitled to medical information
- Questioning can be discriminatory

---

## Documentation Best Practices

### When to Document

**Document:**
- Accommodation requests and responses
- Accessibility barriers reported
- Remediation efforts
- Training completed
- Accessibility checks performed

**Why:**
- Demonstrates good faith effort
- Protects against liability
- Tracks progress
- Identifies patterns

---

### What to Document

**For accommodation requests:**
- Date of request
- Nature of request
- Response provided
- Timeline
- Follow-up

**For accessibility issues:**
- Issue identified
- Date reported
- Remediation plan
- Date resolved
- Person responsible

---

## Accessibility Coordinator

**Every city must have an ADA Coordinator.**

**Their role:**
- Coordinate compliance efforts
- Handle accommodation requests
- Investigate complaints
- Provide training
- Develop policies

**Your role:**
- Know who your ADA coordinator is
- Report accessibility issues to them
- Consult them for complex accommodation requests
- Participate in accessibility initiatives

**Manitou Springs ADA Coordinator:**
[Contact information should be inserted here]

---

## Self-Evaluation and Transition Plan

**Required by ADA:**
Cities must:
1. **Conduct self-evaluation** of all programs and services
2. **Develop transition plan** for addressing barriers
3. **Update regularly**

**What this means for you:**
- Participate in accessibility audits
- Report barriers you identify
- Implement changes as directed
- Track progress

---

## Website Accessibility Compliance

### Legal Standard

**Courts have ruled:**
- Government websites must comply with WCAG 2.0 Level AA (minimum)
- WCAG 2.1 Level AA is current best practice
- No exemption for third-party content or vendors

---

### Common Website Violations

**Top issues in lawsuits:**
1. Missing alt text on images
2. Inaccessible PDFs
3. Forms without labels
4. Videos without captions
5. Low color contrast
6. Keyboard navigation issues
7. Inaccessible third-party widgets

**Your responsibility:**
- Add alt text to all images you upload
- Create accessible PDFs (or use web pages instead)
- Test forms for accessibility
- Add captions to videos
- Check color contrast

---

## Employment Obligations

**ADA also covers employment:**

**Employers must:**
- Provide reasonable accommodations to employees
- Make hiring process accessible
- Ensure workplace is accessible
- Not discriminate in hiring, promotion, or termination

**Examples:**
- Accessible job application process
- Assistive technology for employees
- Modified work schedules
- Accessible break rooms and restrooms

---

## Quick Compliance Checklist

**For every document you create:**
- [ ] Used proper headings
- [ ] Added alt text to images
- [ ] Made links descriptive
- [ ] Checked color contrast
- [ ] Ran accessibility checker

**For every meeting you organize:**
- [ ] Chose accessible venue
- [ ] Provided accommodation notice
- [ ] Arranged for captions/interpreters
- [ ] Prepared accessible materials

**For every web page you publish:**
- [ ] Added alt text to images
- [ ] Used proper heading structure
- [ ] Ensured keyboard accessibility
- [ ] Checked color contrast
- [ ] Tested with accessibility tools

**For every customer interaction:**
- [ ] Provided equal service
- [ ] Respected service animals
- [ ] Offered accommodations
- [ ] Communicated effectively

---

## What to Do If...

### Someone Requests an Accommodation

1. **Listen** to the request
2. **Don't ask** about their disability
3. **Provide** the accommodation if possible
4. **If unsure,** consult your ADA coordinator
5. **Document** the request and response
6. **Follow up** to ensure it worked

---

### You Identify an Accessibility Barrier

1. **Report** to your supervisor or ADA coordinator
2. **Document** the barrier
3. **Suggest** solutions if you have ideas
4. **Follow up** to ensure it's addressed

---

### Someone Files a Complaint

1. **Don't panic** or get defensive
2. **Take it seriously**
3. **Report** to your supervisor immediately
4. **Cooperate** with investigation
5. **Learn** from the situation

---

## Remember

**Accessibility is a civil right, not a favor.**

Compliance isn't just about avoiding lawsuits—it's about ensuring equal access for all residents.

---

## Resources

- **ADA Information:** [ADA.gov](https://www.ada.gov/)
- **ADA Title II:** [Title II Primer](https://www.ada.gov/topics/title-ii/)
- **WCAG Guidelines:** [WCAG 2.1](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/quickref/)
- **Section 508:** [Section508.gov](https://www.section508.gov/)
- **OCR Complaints:** [HHS Office for Civil Rights](https://www.hhs.gov/ocr/index.html)

---

**Questions?**
Contact your ADA Coordinator or HR Department.
